style: Dry, high acidity, light-medium body, unoaked or minimal oak. Pale lemon colour, citrus and green apple primary aromas, possible lees/mineral impression.

nose: Possible wet stone or lees notes
palate: No obvious oak or butter notes
conclusions: Good to Very Good quality (when well-made)
Drink now; may age 3-5 years in better vintages

common_student_errors: Calling it Sauvignon Blanc automatically due to green aromas
Ignoring lack of herbaceous intensity as diagnostic
Marking quality as low because fruit is restrained
Missing mineral/lees character as positive indicator

misconceptions_addressed.correction: Chablis exemplifies Chardonnay in its most neutral form, relying on variety and terroir rather than winemaking

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Low aroma intensity means low quality
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Restrained, precise aromatics combined with high acidity and good structure indicate quality in cool-climate whites

misconceptions_addressed.misconception: Chablis and Sauvignon Blanc are confused
misconceptions_addressed.correction: Chablis (Chardonnay) shows citrus without herbaceous intensity; Sauvignon Blanc shows tropical/herbaceous notes

coaching_hints: Is the wine neutral, or is it precise and balanced?
Notice the acidity on the finish — does it support or undermine quality?
Can you identify primary fruit without secondary winemaking notes?
What role does the cool climate play in the final structure?

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: High acidity and restrained fruit maturity
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Chablis shows vibrant acidity and citrus rather than ripe stone fruit

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Neutral aromatic profile without butter or toast
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Pure varietal character without secondary complexity

causal_reasoning_paths.effect: Mineral or flinty character in nose and palate
causal_reasoning_paths.example_in_wine: Possible wet stone or chalk impressions
